verdict_reasoning: BAX is a BCL2 family pro-apoptotic regulator that functions primarily as a mitochondrial inner-membrane protein and cytoplasmic protein. Its canonical role involves oligomerization in the outer mitochondrial membrane (OMM) to form pores that release cytochrome c during apoptosis. While BAX can translocate to the OMM upon apoptotic signaling, it is not a stably surface-anchored protein accessible from the extracellular face of the plasma membrane. It lacks a transmembrane domain anchoring it to the PM, a GPI anchor, or any other mechanism for stable extracellular-face presentation. BAX is not a target of cell-surface-directed therapeutics (antibodies, ADCs, CAR-T, bispecifics) nor does it have documented constitutive or inducible PM localization via its own mechanism. The protein remains compartmentalized to the cytosol and mitochondria.
